title: What to know before using Anthemis cotula (Mayweed)?
intro: Anthemis cotula, also known as stinking chamomile, is a plant with a variety of medicinal properties. It has been used in traditional medicine for its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and antibacterial properties. The plant contains several active compounds, including azulenes and flavonoids, which are believed to be responsible for its medicinal effects. In terms of horticultural conditions, Anthemis cotula is a hardy annual or short-lived perennial that thrives in full sun and well-drained soil. It can be found growing wild in fields, meadows, and along roadsides. The plant grows to be 1-2 feet tall and has finely divided, feathery leaves and small white or yellow daisy-like flowers. Despite its medicinal uses, Anthemis cotula is considered a weed in many areas due to its ability to spread rapidly and take over gardens and natural areas.
medicine_desc: Anthemis cotula, also known as stinking chamomile or mayweed, is a plant that has been used for its medicinal properties for centuries. The plant has a long history of use in traditional medicine, particularly in the treatment of skin conditions, inflammation, and respiratory issues. One of the main benefits of Anthemis cotula is its anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to reduce redness, swelling, and pain in affected areas. Additionally, the plant has been shown to have antibacterial and antifungal properties, making it useful in the treatment of wounds, cuts, and infections. Furthermore, Anthemis cotula has been used to treat digestive issues such as indigestion, bloating, and nausea. The plant's volatile oils, including bisabolol and chamazulene, are thought to be responsible for its medicinal effects.

Anthemis cotula contains a number of active constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. The plant's essential oil is rich in volatile compounds such as bisabolol, chamazulene, and alpha-pinene, which have anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal effects. The plant also contains flavonoids, such as apigenin and luteolin, which have antioxidant properties and may help to protect cells from damage. Additionally, Anthemis cotula contains coumarins, which have been shown to have anticoagulant effects. The plant's constituents work together to provide a range of medicinal benefits, making it a valuable tool in natural medicine.

There are several ways to prepare Anthemis cotula for medicinal use. One common preparation is as a tincture, which is made by soaking the plant's flowers and leaves in alcohol. The resulting solution can be taken orally to treat digestive issues, or applied topically to treat skin conditions and inflammation. The plant can also be made into a salve or ointment by combining the essential oil with a base such as beeswax. This preparation can be applied directly to the skin to treat wounds, cuts, and other skin irritations. Additionally, Anthemis cotula can be used in a compress or poultice to treat inflammation and pain.

Despite its many benefits, Anthemis cotula can also have some side effects, particularly if it is not used properly. The plant's essential oil is highly concentrated and can cause skin irritation, redness, and burning if applied directly to the skin. Additionally, the plant can cause allergic reactions in some people, leading to symptoms such as hives, itching, and difficulty breathing. Ingesting large amounts of the plant can also be harmful, causing symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. It is important to use Anthemis cotula in moderation and to follow proper preparation and dosage instructions.

When using Anthemis cotula, it is important to take certain precautions to ensure safe and effective use. The plant should not be used by pregnant or breastfeeding women, as it can stimulate menstruation and potentially cause miscarriage. Additionally, the plant should be used with caution in people with known allergies to ragweed or other members of the Asteraceae family. It is also important to use only high-quality, properly prepared Anthemis cotula products, as improper preparation can lead to contamination and potentially harmful side effects. Finally, it is always a good idea to consult with a healthcare provider before starting any new natural medicine regimen.
horticulture_desc: Anthemis cotula, also known as Mayweed or Dog Fennel, is a hardy annual plant that is native to Europe and parts of Asia. It typically grows to a height of 30-60 cm and has a spread of 45-60 cm. This plant prefers full sun and well-drained soil, and it can tolerate a wide range of soil types, from sandy to clay. Anthemis cotula is drought tolerant and can thrive in poor soil conditions, making it a low-maintenance option for gardeners. However, it does not tolerate wet or waterlogged soils. It is also intolerant of shade, so it should be planted in an area that receives at least 6 hours of sunlight per day.

When planting Anthemis cotula, it is best to sow the seeds directly into the garden in the spring, after the last frost date. The seeds should be sown thinly, about 2.5 cm apart, and covered with a thin layer of soil. The seeds will germinate in 14-21 days. If starting the seeds indoors, they should be sown in late winter or early spring, about 6-8 weeks before the last frost date. The seedlings should be transplanted into the garden after the last frost date, spacing them about 30-45 cm apart.

Anthemis cotula is a low-maintenance plant that does not require a lot of care. However, it does benefit from regular watering, especially during dry spells. The plant should be watered deeply, allowing the water to reach the roots. It is important to avoid overwatering, as this can lead to root rot. The plant also benefits from regular deadheading, which encourages new blooms and keeps the plant looking tidy. Fertilizer is not necessary, as Anthemis cotula is able to thrive in poor soil conditions.

Anthemis cotula is typically grown for its attractive, yellow, daisy-like flowers. The flowers are produced in abundance throughout the summer and are highly attractive to bees and butterflies. The flowers can be harvested for use in flower arrangements or for drying. To dry the flowers, cut the stems when the flowers are in full bloom and hang them upside down in a dark, dry, well-ventilated area. The flowers should be completely dry in about two weeks.

Anthemis cotula is relatively pest and disease-free, but it can be susceptible to powdery mildew, especially in humid conditions. To prevent powdery mildew, it is important to ensure that the plant has good air circulation and to avoid overwatering. If powdery mildew does occur, it can be treated with a fungicide. The plant can also be affected by aphids, which can be controlled by introducing beneficial insects such as ladybugs or by using insecticidal soap. Slugs and snails can also be a problem, but they can be controlled by using slug pellets or by creating a barrier around the plant with sharp grit or eggshells.
botany_desc: Anthemis cotula, commonly known as stinking chamomile or mayweed, is a species of flowering plant in the daisy family, Asteraceae. It is native to Europe and North Africa but has been introduced and naturalized in many parts of the world, including North America. Anthemis cotula is an annual plant that grows to a height of 30-60 cm and has a taproot system. It is a member of the tribe Anthemideae, which includes other chamomile species. The genus Anthemis contains about 200 species, but Anthemis cotula is one of the most widely distributed and invasive.

Anthemis cotula has a characteristic morphology that distinguishes it from other Anthemis species. It has a branched stem that is covered with fine hairs, and its leaves are alternate, bi-pinnate, and also covered with hairs. The leaves have a strong, unpleasant odor when crushed, which gives the plant its common name of stinking chamomile. The flowers are small, white, and clustered in terminal heads. Each flower head has a center disk of tiny tubular florets surrounded by white ray florets. The flowers bloom from June to October and are attractive to bees and butterflies.

Anthemis cotula has several variant names, including dog fennel, stinkweed, and mayweed. These names reflect the plant's strong odor and its ability to invade crops and pastures. Anthemis cotula is often mistaken for other Anthemis species, such as Anthemis nobilis, the Roman chamomile, or Anthemis tinctoria, the golden chamomile. However, Anthemis cotula can be distinguished by its stronger odor, finer hairs on the stem and leaves, and smaller flower heads. Anthemis cotula is also more invasive and aggressive than other Anthemis species, making it a problematic weed in many areas.

Anthemis cotula is native to Europe and North Africa but has been introduced and naturalized in many parts of the world, including North America, Australia, and New Zealand. It prefers disturbed habitats, such as roadsides, fields, and waste areas, and can tolerate a wide range of soil types and conditions. Anthemis cotula is often found in areas with dry summers and mild winters, such as the Mediterranean region. It can also be found in coastal areas, where it can tolerate salt spray and sandy soils. Anthemis cotula is a successful invasive species due to its ability to produce large numbers of seeds and its tolerance of a wide range of environmental conditions.

Anthemis cotula has a life cycle that is typical of annual plants. It reproduces by seed, and each plant can produce up to 20,000 seeds. The seeds are small, brown, and have a feathery pappus that aids in wind dispersal. The seeds can remain viable in the soil for up to five years, making Anthemis cotula a persistent and difficult-to-control weed. Anthemis cotula germinates in the spring and produces flowers and seeds in the summer. The seeds are mature in the fall and are dispersed by the wind, often in the late summer and early fall. Anthemis cotula can also reproduce vegetatively, by fragmentation of the root system, which can lead to the formation of new plants. This ability to reproduce both sexually and asexually makes Anthemis cotula a successful and adaptable species.
